explanation: The Badrinath Temple, located in the town of Badrinath in Uttarakhand, is dedicated to Lord Vishnu (worshipped as Badrinarayan). It is one of the Char Dham and Chota Char Dham pilgrimage sites. The image of the presiding deity is a black stone statue of Vishnu in a meditative posture (Badri Vishal). The temple is situated on the banks of the Alaknanda River. Like Kedarnath, it remains closed during the winter months.

question: 'Vaishno Devi Temple' is located in which state/UT?
explanation: The Vaishno Devi Temple is a holy cave shrine located in the Trikuta Mountains of Jammu and Kashmir (specifically in Katra, Reasi district). It is dedicated to Mata Vaishno Devi (a manifestation of the Hindu Mother Goddess Mahalakshmi). The shrine contains three natural rock formations called Pindis, representing Maha Kali, Maha Lakshmi, and Maha Saraswati. Millions of pilgrims undertake the 12-13 km trek from Katra to the Bhawan annually.

question: 'Siddhivinayak Temple' is located in which city?
explanation: The Shree Siddhivinayak Ganapati Mandir is a famous Hindu temple dedicated to Lord Ganesha. It is located in the Prabhadevi area of Mumbai, Maharashtra. Originally built in 1801 by Laxman Vithu and Deubai Patil, the temple has grown into one of the richest and most frequented temples in Mumbai. The idol of Ganesha here is unique as the trunk is tilted to the right (Siddhi Vinayak), symbolizing the granting of boons (Siddhi).

question: 'Kamakhya Temple' is located in which state?
explanation: The Kamakhya Temple is situated on the Nilachal Hill in Guwahati, Assam. It is one of the oldest and most revered of the 51 Shakti Peethas. The temple is dedicated to the Goddess Kamakhya (the bleeding Goddess). It is unique because it celebrates the ability of the goddess to conceive; the temple has no idol but a yoni-shaped stone that is worshipped. It hosts the famous annual Ambubachi Mela, which celebrates the menstruation of the goddess.

question: 'Lotus Temple' is associated with which faith?
explanation: The Lotus Temple, located in New Delhi, is a Bahá'í House of Worship. Completed in 1986, it is notable for its flower-like shape, composed of 27 free-standing marble-clad "petals" arranged in clusters of three to form nine sides. The Bahá'í faith teaches the oneness of God, the oneness of religions, and the oneness of humanity. The temple is open to all, regardless of religion or any other qualification, serving as a space for meditation and prayer.

question: 'Akshardham Temple' is located in which city?
explanation: The Swaminarayan Akshardham complex is a massive Hindu temple complex located in Delhi (on the banks of the Yamuna River). Opened in 2005, it displays millennia of traditional Hindu and Indian culture, spirituality, and architecture. It was built by the BAPS Swaminarayan Sanstha under the guidance of Pramukh Swami Maharaj. The central monument is built of Rajasthani pink sandstone and Italian Carrara marble without the use of ferrous metal (steel/iron) in its structure.

question: 'Virupaksha Temple' is located in which state?
explanation: The Virupaksha Temple is located in Hampi, Karnataka. It is part of the Group of Monuments at Hampi, a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Dedicated to Lord Shiva (known here as Virupaksha, consort of the local goddess Pampa), the temple predates the Vijayanagara Empire but was expanded significantly under their rule, especially by King Krishnadevaraya who built the main Gopuram. It is one of the few temples in Hampi that has functioned continuously since its construction in the 7th century.

question: 'Gomateshwara Statue' is located in which town?
explanation: The Gomateshwara Statue (also known as the Bahubali statue) is a 57-foot (17 m) high monolithic statue located on Vindyagiri Hill in Shravanabelagola, Karnataka. It was built around 983 AD by Chavundaraya, a general of the Ganga dynasty. It is one of the most important pilgrimage sites for Jains, dedicated to Bahubali, the son of the first Tirthankara Rishabhanatha. Every 12 years, the Mahamastakabhisheka festival is held here, where the statue is anointed with milk, saffron, and ghee.

question: 'Padmanabhaswamy Temple' is located in which city?
explanation: The Sree Padmanabhaswamy Temple is located in Thiruvananthapuram, the capital of Kerala. The city itself is named after the deity ("Thiru-Anantha-Puram" means City of Lord Ananta). The temple is dedicated to Lord Vishnu, who is depicted reclining on the serpent Anantha (Adi Shesha). It is famous for being the richest place of worship in the world following the discovery of immense treasure (gold, gems) in its secret vaults (Kallaras) in 2011. Its architecture is a fusion of Kerala and Dravidian styles.

question: 'Ramanathaswamy Temple' is located in which island?
explanation: The Ramanathaswamy Temple is a Hindu temple dedicated to Lord Shiva located on Rameswaram Island (Pamban Island) in Tamil Nadu. It is one of the twelve Jyotirlinga temples and a Char Dham pilgrimage site. According to the Ramayana, the Lingam here was established and worshipped by Lord Rama before he crossed the bridge to Lanka. The temple is renowned for its magnificent corridors with massive sculpted pillars, which are the longest temple corridors in the world.

question: 'Dwarkadhish Temple' is located in which state?
explanation: The Dwarkadhish Temple (Jagat Mandir) is located in Dwarka, Gujarat. It is dedicated to Lord Krishna, who is worshipped here as the "King of Dwarka" (Dwarkadhish). It is one of the Char Dham pilgrimage sites. The current structure, a 5-storied building supported by 72 pillars, was built around the 16th century in the Chalukya style, though the original temple is believed to have been built by Krishna's grandson, Vajranabha, over Krishna's residential palace (Hari-Griha).

question: 'Lingaraja Temple' is located in which city?
explanation: The Lingaraja Temple is the largest and oldest temple in Bhubaneswar, Odisha (often called the "City of Temples"). Dedicated to Lord Shiva (Harihara), it represents the quintessence of Kalinga architecture. Built in the 11th century by the Somavamsi dynasty kings, the main tower (Vimana) rises to 180 feet. The deity is worshipped as Tribhuvaneshwara (Master of Three Worlds) and is a unique composite form of Vishnu and Shiva (Harihara), signified by the flag atop the temple holding both a Trident and a Chakra.

question: 'Mahabodhi Temple' is located in which city?
explanation: The Mahabodhi Temple (Temple of Great Awakening) is a UNESCO World Heritage Site located in Bodh Gaya, Bihar. It marks the location where the Buddha is said to have attained Enlightenment (Bodhi). The temple complex includes the main temple (built in the classical Gupta style) and the sacred Bodhi Tree (a descendant of the original tree under which Buddha sat). It is one of the four holy sites related to the life of the Lord Buddha and a major pilgrimage destination for Buddhists worldwide.

question: Who is the author of 'God of Small Things'?
explanation: Arundhati Roy is the author of the debut novel 'The God of Small Things', published in 1997. The book is a family drama set in Ayemenem, Kerala, exploring the tragic decline of an affluent Syrian Christian family. It won the prestigious Booker Prize for Fiction in 1997, making Roy the first Indian citizen to win the award. The novel is celebrated for its non-sequential narrative and lush, atmospheric prose that delves into themes of casteism (Love Laws), social discrimination, and the loss of innocence.

question: Who wrote the book 'Train to Pakistan'?
explanation: 'Train to Pakistan' is a historical novel by Khushwant Singh, published in 1956. It is widely regarded as one of the most powerful and defining novels about the Partition of India in 1947. Set in the fictional village of Mano Majra on the India-Pakistan border, the story vividly depicts the communal violence and human tragedy that unfolded during the mass migration. Instead of focusing on political events, Singh humanizes the conflict through the lives of ordinary villagers, highlighting the absurdity and horror of religious hatred.

question: Who is the author of 'The White Tiger'?
explanation: Aravind Adiga is the author of the debut novel 'The White Tiger', published in 2008. It won the 40th Man Booker Prize in the same year. The novel creates a darkly humorous perspective of India's class struggle in a globalized world as told through a retrospective narration from Balram Halwai, a village boy who rises to become a successful entrepreneur in Bangalore. The book offers a scathing critique of the Indian caste system, poverty, and corruption, contrasting the "Darkness" of rural India with the "Light" of urban success.

question: Who wrote 'Midnight's Children'?
explanation: 'Midnight's Children' is a landmark novel by Salman Rushdie, published in 1981. It deals with India's transition from British colonialism to independence and the partition of India. The protagonist, Saleem Sinai, is born at the exact moment of India's independence (midnight, August 15, 1947) and possesses telepathic powers linking him to other "midnight's children." The book won both the Booker Prize in 1981 and the Booker of Bookers (1993 and 2008), celebrating it as the best novel to win the prize in its first 25 and 40 years.

question: Who is the author of 'A Suitable Boy'?
explanation: 'A Suitable Boy' is a monumental novel by Vikram Seth, published in 1993. It is one of the longest novels ever published in a single volume in the English language. Set in a post-independence, post-partition India (early 1950s), the story revolves around Mrs. Rupa Mehra's efforts to find a "suitable boy" for her daughter, Lata. Through four prominent families, Seth explores a panoramic view of India's political, social, and religious landscape during the first general elections and the abolition of the Zamindari system.

question: Who wrote 'The Guide'?
explanation: 'The Guide' is a classic novel written by R.K. Narayan, published in 1958. Set in the fictional town of Malgudi, it tells the story of Raju, a tour guide who transforms from a corrupt man into a spiritual guide (holy man) and ultimately fasts to bring rain to a drought-stricken village. The novel won the Sahitya Akademi Award in 1960, making Narayan the first Indian author writing in English to receive this honor. It was also adapted into a highly successful Bollywood film starring Dev Anand.

question: Who is the author of 'Interpreter of Maladies'?
explanation: 'Interpreter of Maladies' is a collection of nine short stories by Indian-American author Jhumpa Lahiri, published in 1999. The book won the Pulitzer Prize for Fiction in 2000. The stories navigate the complex emotional lives of Indians and Indian Americans who are caught between the traditions of their ancestors and the baffling "New World." Themes of marital difficulties, the trials of immigration, and the poignant disconnect between generations are central to the collection, showcasing Lahiri's elegant and understated prose style.

question: Who wrote 'The Inheritance of Loss'?
explanation: 'The Inheritance of Loss' is a novel by Kiran Desai, published in 2006. It won the Man Booker Prize that same year. Set in the Himalayas (Kalimpong) and New York City, the story revolves around a retired judge, his granddaughter Sai, and their cook. Against the backdrop of the Gorkhaland movement in the 1980s, the novel explores themes of migration, living between two worlds, and the lingering effects of colonialism. Kiran Desai is the daughter of the famous novelist Anita Desai.

question: Who is the author of 'Train to Pakistan'?
explanation: (Note: This is a repeated question from a different exam shift). Khushwant Singh authored the seminal novel 'Train to Pakistan'. Apart from being a novelist, Singh was a renowned journalist, lawyer, and politician. He was known for his wit, secularism, and column "With Malice Towards One and All." The novel remains his most enduring literary contribution, capturing the raw horror of the partition riots where a "ghost train" full of corpses arrives in a peaceful village, shattering its harmony forever.

question: Who wrote 'Discovery of India'?
explanation: 'The Discovery of India' was written by Pandit Jawaharlal Nehru, India's first Prime Minister, during his imprisonment at the Ahmednagar Fort from 1942 to 1946 (during the Quit India Movement). Published in 1946, the book is a sweeping history of India starting from the Indus Valley Civilization to the British Raj. It analyzes Indian philosophy, art, and culture, arguing that India is a historic nation with a right to sovereignty. It was later adapted into the famous TV series Bharat Ek Khoj.

question: Who is the author of 'My Experiments with Truth'?
explanation: 'The Story of My Experiments with Truth' is the autobiography of Mohandas Karamchand Gandhi (Mahatma Gandhi). Originally written in Gujarati (Satya Na Prayogo), it was serialized in his journal Navjivan. It covers his life from early childhood through to 1921. The book details his moral and spiritual development, his struggles with vegetarianism, celibacy (Brahmacharya), and truth (Satya), and the evolution of his philosophy of non-violent resistance (Satyagraha). It remains a global classic on spiritual resilience.

question: Who wrote 'Gitanjali'?
explanation: 'Gitanjali' (Song Offerings) is a collection of poems by Rabindranath Tagore. The original Bengali collection was published in 1910. Tagore translated the poems into English prose, which was published in 1912 with an introduction by W.B. Yeats. This work earned Tagore the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1913, making him the first non-European to win the prize. The poems are deeply spiritual and devotional, exploring the relationship between the poet and the divine.

question: Who is the author of 'Wings of Fire'?
explanation: 'Wings of Fire: An Autobiography' was written by Dr. A.P.J. Abdul Kalam, the former President of India, along with Arun Tiwari. Published in 1999, the book narrates Kalam's early life, his humble beginnings in Rameswaram, and his journey to becoming a key scientist in Indian space research (ISRO) and missile programs (DRDO). It provides a detailed account of the development of the Agni and Prithvi missiles, inspiring millions of youth with its message of perseverance and visionary thinking.

question: Who wrote 'An Era of Darkness'?
explanation: 'An Era of Darkness: The British Empire in India' (published internationally as Inglorious Empire) is a non-fiction book by Shashi Tharoor, published in 2016. The book offers a scathing critique of British colonial rule in India, arguing that it devastated the Indian economy and society rather than developing it. It covers topics like the de-industrialization of textiles, the Bengal Famine, and the railways being built for British benefit. Tharoor won the Sahitya Akademi Award (English) in 2019 for this book.

question: Who is the author of 'The Accidental Prime Minister'?
explanation: 'The Accidental Prime Minister: The Making and Unmaking of Manmohan Singh' is a memoir by Sanjaya Baru, published in 2014. Baru served as the media advisor to Prime Minister Manmohan Singh from 2004 to 2008. The book provides a behind-the-scenes look at the functioning of the UPA government and the dynamics between the Prime Minister's Office (PMO) and the Congress party leadership. It generated significant political controversy and was later adapted into a film of the same name.

question: Who wrote 'Playing It My Way'?
explanation: 'Playing It My Way' is the autobiography of the legendary Indian cricketer Sachin Tendulkar. Co-authored with sports historian Boria Majumdar, it was published in 2014. The book chronicles Tendulkar's illustrious 24-year career, from his early days as a child prodigy to becoming the highest run-scorer in international cricket. It offers insights into his most memorable innings, the 2011 World Cup victory, and personal anecdotes about teammates and family, breaking the sales records for an adult hardback in India upon release.

question: Who is the author of 'Unbreakable'?
explanation: 'Unbreakable: An Autobiography' is the life story of the celebrated Indian boxer Mary Kom (Mangte Chungneijang Mary Kom). Published in 2013, the book details her relentless struggle from a humble background in rural Manipur to becoming a five-time World Amateur Boxing Champion and an Olympic Bronze medallist (London 2012). It highlights her determination to overcome poverty, gender bias, and the challenges of balancing motherhood with an elite sporting career.

question: Who wrote 'The Test of My Life'?
explanation: 'The Test of My Life: From Cricket to Cancer and Back' is the autobiography of Indian cricketer Yuvraj Singh. The book is a poignant account of his battle with a rare form of cancer (mediastinal seminoma) shortly after being the Player of the Tournament in India's 2011 World Cup win. It details the physical and emotional agony of chemotherapy in the USA and his inspiring return to international cricket, serving as a beacon of hope for cancer survivors.

question: Who is the author of 'Ace Against Odds'?
explanation: 'Ace Against Odds' is the autobiography of Indian tennis star Sania Mirza, co-authored with her father, Imran Mirza. Published in 2016, the book narrates her journey to becoming the World No. 1 in women's doubles. It discusses the controversies she faced throughout her career—from fatwas issued against her attire to questions about her patriotism after marrying Pakistani cricketer Shoaib Malik—and how she overcame these odds to succeed on the court.

question: Who wrote 'Straight from the Heart'?
explanation: 'Straight from the Heart' is an autobiography of Kapil Dev, India's legendary all-rounder and the captain of the 1983 World Cup-winning team. In this book, the "Haryana Hurricane" speaks candidly about his cricketing philosophy, his love for the game, and his experiences leading the Indian team. It reflects his simple, honest, and passionate nature. He has also authored other books like By God's Decree and Cricket My Style.

question: Who is the author of 'Sunny Days'?
explanation: 'Sunny Days' is the autobiography of Sunil Gavaskar, one of the greatest opening batsmen in cricket history. Published in 1976 while he was still an active player, the book covers his early life and his meteoric rise in international cricket, including his record-breaking debut series against the West Indies in 1971. It is considered a classic in cricket literature for its insightful analysis and articulate writing style. He later wrote sequels like Idols and Runs 'n Ruins.

question: Who wrote 'A Century Is Not Enough'?
explanation: 'A Century Is Not Enough: My Roller-coaster Ride to Success' is the memoir of former Indian cricket captain Sourav Ganguly, co-authored with Gautam Bhattacharya. Published in 2018, the book details Ganguly's journey from his debut at Lord's to becoming one of India's most successful captains. It frankly discusses his turbulent phases, including his controversial ouster from the team during the Greg Chappell era and his determined comeback, offering lessons on leadership and resilience.

question: Who is the author of '281 and Beyond'?
explanation: '281 and Beyond' is the autobiography of elegant Indian batsman V.V.S. Laxman, co-written with R. Kaushik. The title refers to his historic innings of 281 runs against Australia at Eden Gardens, Kolkata, in 2001, which is widely regarded as one of the greatest test innings played under pressure. The book explores the mental aspect of the game, his partnerships with Rahul Dravid, and his decision to retire, providing a deep dive into the life of a "Very Very Special" cricketer.

question: Who wrote 'Mind Master'?
explanation: 'Mind Master: Winning Lessons from a Champion's Life' is the autobiography of India's first Grandmaster and five-time World Chess Champion, Viswanathan Anand. Co-authored with Susan Ninan, the book uses his greatest games as metaphors to discuss life lessons on memory, tactics, navigating failure, and handling success. It offers a glimpse into the psychological warfare of elite chess and how Anand evolved his game to stay relevant against computer engines and younger rivals.

question: Who is the author of 'No Spin'?
explanation: 'No Spin' is the autobiography of the late Australian leg-spin legend Shane Warne. Published in 2018, the book is a brutally honest account of his life on and off the field. Warne discusses his mastery of the art of leg-spin, the "Ball of the Century," his bans, scandals, and his relationships. The title is a play on words, referring to his bowling style and his promise to tell the truth without "spinning" the facts.

question: Who wrote 'Six Machine'?
explanation: 'Six Machine: I Don't Like Cricket... I Love It' is the autobiography of the West Indian cricketing superstar Chris Gayle. Known as the "Universe Boss," Gayle is famous for his explosive batting in T20 cricket. The book chronicles his journey from a tin-roofed shack in Kingston, Jamaica, to becoming a global T20 freelancer. It reflects his flamboyant personality and his unapologetic approach to life and the game.

question: Who is the author of 'The Race of My Life'?
explanation: 'The Race of My Life' is the autobiography of Milkha Singh, popularly known as the "Flying Sikh." Co-authored with his daughter Sonia Sanwalka, the book details his traumatic childhood during the Partition, his time in the army, and his rise to becoming India's greatest sprinter. It famously recounts his heartbreaking fourth-place finish at the 1960 Rome Olympics by 0.1 seconds. The Bollywood biographical film Bhaag Milkha Bhaag is based on this book.

question: Who wrote 'Golden Girl'?
explanation: 'Golden Girl' is the autobiography of P.T. Usha (Pilavullakandi Thekkeparambil Usha), the queen of Indian track and field. Written with Lokesh Sharma, the book narrates her journey from the Payyoli village in Kerala to dominating the Asian track scene in the 1980s. It covers her training under coach O.M. Nambiar and her near-miss at a medal in the 1984 Los Angeles Olympics, where she lost the Bronze in the 400m hurdles by 1/100th of a second.

question: Who is the author of 'Goal'?
explanation: 'Goal' is the autobiography of Major Dhyan Chand, widely regarded as the greatest field hockey player in history. The book details his career, spanning from his army days to winning three consecutive Olympic Gold medals for India (1928, 1932, and 1936). Known as the "Wizard of Hockey," Dhyan Chand writes about his ball control, the golden era of Indian hockey, and his experiences playing against Hitler's Germany in the Berlin Olympics.

question: Which city is home to the cricket ground, Wankhede Stadium?
explanation: The Wankhede Stadium is an international cricket stadium located in Mumbai, Maharashtra. It is the home ground of the Mumbai Indians (IPL team) and the Mumbai Ranji team. Built in 1974, it is famous for hosting the 2011 Cricket World Cup Final, where India defeated Sri Lanka to lift the trophy after 28 years. The stadium is known for its sea breeze (being near the Arabian Sea) which aids swing bowlers and its vibrant atmosphere due to the stands being close to the playing area.

question: What does the term "checkmate" refer to in chess?
explanation: In chess, "Checkmate" (often shortened to mate) is a game position in which a player's King is in check (threatened with capture) and there is no legal move to remove the threat. Checkmate ends the game immediately, and the player whose King is checkmated loses. The term comes from the Persian phrase "Shah Mat", meaning "the King is helpless/defeated." It is distinct from a "Stalemate," which results in a draw when the King is not in check but has no legal moves.

question: What is the radius of the centre circle in a football field?
explanation: According to the Laws of the Game established by IFAB (International Football Association Board), the radius of the centre circle on a standard football pitch is 9.15 meters (which equals 10 yards). This circle marks the area where opposing players must remain outside during the kick-off until the ball is in play. The 9.15m distance is a standard measurement in football, also used for the distance of the defensive wall from a free kick and the arc outside the penalty box.

question: What is the radius of the striking or shooting circle in hockey?
explanation: In field hockey, the striking circle (or shooting circle/D-circle) is marked by a solid line. The radius of this semi-circle is 14.63 meters (exactly 16 yards) measured from the inner edge of the goal posts. A goal can only be scored if an attacker strikes the ball from within this semi-circular area. The area also includes a dotted line 5 meters outside the circle. (Note: 16 yards = 14.63 meters).

question: What is the standard width of a hockey field?
explanation: A standard field hockey pitch is rectangular in shape. The length (side lines) is 91.40 meters (100 yards), and the width (back lines) is 55.00 meters (60 yards). These dimensions are standardized by the International Hockey Federation (FIH). The field is divided by a center line and two 23-meter lines. Modern hockey is predominantly played on artificial turf (astro-turf), which allows for faster gameplay compared to grass fields.

question: In Kho-Kho, how many players are in a team, and how many are on the field during a game?
options: 9 players in a team, 9 on the field.
12 players in a team, 9 on the field.
12 players in a team, 12 on the field.
9 players in a team, 12 on the field.
explanation: In a standard Kho-Kho match, each team consists of 12 players. However, only 9 players take the field for a contest. The chasing team sends 9 players onto the field (8 sit in the central lane, 1 is the active chaser), while the defending team sends runners in batches of 3. The remaining 3 squad members are substitutes. Kho-Kho is a traditional Indian tag sport that tests speed, agility, and tactical thinking.

question: The first known rules of Kabaddi were framed in which Indian state?
explanation: The first known modern rules of Kabaddi were framed in Maharashtra around 1921-1923 by the Deccan Gymkhana of Pune and the Hind Vijay Gymkhana of Baroda (Gujarat/Maharashtra region). In 1923, these rules were published and used during the All India Kabaddi Tournament. The Amateur Kabaddi Federation of India (AKFI) was later founded in 1973 to regulate the sport. While Kabaddi has ancient roots across India (known as Chedugudu, Hu-Tu-Tu), the standardized competitive format originated in Maharashtra.

question: Which of the following statements about Judo is correct?
options: Kalpana Devi won gold at 2010 World Cup
Introduced in 1882 by Jigoro Kano in Japan
explanation: Statement (a) is correct: Judo ("the gentle way") was created as a physical, mental, and moral pedagogy in Japan by Jigoro Kano in 1882. He evolved it from Jujutsu. Statement (d) is incorrect because Judo became an official Olympic sport for men in 1964 (Tokyo Games) and for women in 1992. The first National Championship in India was held in 1966.

question: Mary Kom's most recent international gold medal was won at which tournament?
explanation: Based on the timeline (Mary Kom's active career phases), her significant recent international gold came at the 23rd President's Cup held in Labuan Bajo, Indonesia, in July 2019. She won gold in the 51kg category. While she is a legend with 6 World Championship golds, she did not compete/win gold at the Hangzhou Asian Games (2023) or CWG Birmingham (2022) due to injury/trial issues during the twilight of her career.

question: At the 1960 Rome Olympics, Milkha Singh finished Fourth with a timing of \_\_\_\_\_\_ seconds.
explanation: At the 1960 Rome Olympics, Milkha Singh (The Flying Sikh) finished fourth in the 400m final with a timing of 45.6 seconds (manually timed as 45.6, electronically 45.73). He broke the existing Olympic record but still missed the bronze medal by a fraction of a second (0.1 sec) in a photo-finish race won by Otis Davis. This remains one of the most heartbreaking yet celebrated moments in Indian athletic history.

question: Where is the Dr. S. P. Mookerjee Swimming Stadium located?
explanation: The Dr. Syama Prasad Mookerjee Swimming Pool Complex is located in New Delhi. It was originally built for the 1982 Asian Games and was significantly renovated for the 2010 Commonwealth Games. It is a world-class aquatic complex owned by the Sports Authority of India (SAI). It features an Olympic-sized racing pool, a diving pool, and a warm-up pool, hosting major national and international swimming events.

question: Who scored 10 goals for India in their 24-1 victory over the USA to secure a second consecutive Olympic gold?
explanation: In the 1932 Los Angeles Olympics, the Indian hockey team defeated the USA by a massive margin of 24-1. In this match, Roop Singh (younger brother of Dhyan Chand) scored 10 goals, while Dhyan Chand scored 8 goals. This victory is part of the golden era of Indian hockey. The scoreline remains a world record for the highest number of goals scored in an Olympic hockey match (later equalled but historically significant).

question: In freestyle wrestling, which action can result in a technical point?
explanation: In freestyle wrestling, performing a successful takedown results in the awarding of technical points (usually 2 points). A takedown is when a wrestler takes their opponent to the mat from a standing position and gains control. Actions like running out of the mat deliberately or holding the singlet (options a and b) are considered infractions/fouls and may result in cautions or penalty points awarded to the opponent, not technical points for the doer.

question: Vijender Singh defeated which player in the quarter-finals to secure India's first Olympic boxing medal?
explanation: At the 2008 Beijing Olympics, Vijender Singh created history by defeating Carlos Góngora of Ecuador in the quarter-finals of the Middleweight (75 kg) category. By winning this bout (9-4 on points), he guaranteed himself a spot in the semi-finals and assured India's first-ever Olympic medal (Bronze) in boxing. He eventually lost the semi-final to Emilio Correa of Cuba but had already secured the bronze.

question: Which of the following is a correct statement regarding violations in basketball?
options: Player remains in play on sideline
Player may stay in restricted area for 5s
Dribble ends when both hands touch ball
explanation: Statement (c) is correct: In basketball, a dribble ends when a player touches the ball with both hands simultaneously or permits the ball to come to rest in one or both hands. Resuming dribbling after this is a violation called "Double Dribble." \* (a) is wrong: The limit in the restricted area (key) is 3 seconds. \* (b) is wrong: The shot clock is 24 seconds (8 seconds is to cross the half-court). \* (d) is wrong: Stepping on the sideline is out of bounds.

question: Which of the following statements about sculling in swimming is correct?
options: Maintains balance by moving hands under water
explanation: Sculling is a fundamental swimming technique used to "feel the water." It involves moving the hands back and forth under the water in a figure-eight or infinity motion. This action creates lift and propulsion, helping the swimmer maintain balance, stay afloat (treading water), or move efficiently without using large arm strokes. It is used by both beginners (for safety) and elite artistic swimmers (synchro) for complex maneuvers.

question: Who became India's first Asian Games gold medallist by winning the 100m freestyle in 1951?
explanation: Sachin Nag became India's first-ever gold medallist at the Asian Games by winning the 100m Freestyle swimming event at the inaugural 1951 Asian Games held in New Delhi. He clocked a time of 1 minute 4.7 seconds. Besides swimming, he was also a water polo player and represented India in the 1948 London Olympics in water polo. His achievement laid the foundation for Indian swimming at the continental level.

question: Which historical figure is the Santosh Trophy, launched in 1941, named after?
explanation: The Santosh Trophy, the National Football Championship of India, is named after the late Maharaja Sir Manmatha Nath Roy Chowdhury of Santosh (now in Bangladesh). He was the President of the Indian Football Association (IFA), the organization that donated the trophy. The tournament began in 1941. It remains the premier inter-state football competition in India, where state associations and government institutions compete.

question: What is a "brace" in football?
options: Scoring two goals in a match
explanation: In football (soccer) terminology, scoring two goals in a single match by the same player is called a "brace". \* Hat-trick: Scoring three goals. \* Haul: Scoring four goals. \* Glut: Scoring five goals. The term likely originates from the Old English word brace, meaning a pair (as in a brace of pheasants).

question: Which of the following actions is not allowed for a player in handball?
options: Intercepting ball mid-air with both hands
explanation: In Handball, it is a foul to pull or punch the ball out of the hands of an opponent. Players can block or intercept the ball when it is in the air or being dribbled, but they cannot aggressively snatch or strike it while the opponent is holding it securely. Physical contact must be frontal; hitting, pulling, or endangering the opponent (especially the throwing arm) leads to penalties (free throw, 7-meter throw, or suspension).

question: Match the Taekwon-Do Korean terms with meanings: 1. Tae (C) Strike with feet 2. Kwon (B) Strike with hand 3. Do (A) Discipline/Way 4. Dojang (D) Training Hall
explanation: Taekwondo is a Korean martial art. \* Tae: To strike or break with the foot (kick). \* Kwon: To strike or break with the fist (hand). \* Do: The way, method, or discipline (art). \* Dojang: The hall or place where the "Do" (way) is practiced (Training Hall). Therefore, 1-C, 2-B, 3-A, 4-D is the correct match.

question: In which Indian city is the Subroto Cup conducted every year?
explanation: The Subroto Cup International Football Tournament is a prestigious inter-school football tournament conducted annually in New Delhi. It is named after Air Marshal Subroto Mukerjee, the first Indian Chief of Air Staff. Started in 1960, it is organized by the Subroto Mukerjee Sports Education Society under the aegis of the Indian Air Force. It aims to identify young talent at the grassroots level from schools across India and invited foreign countries.

question: Consider the following statements on World Championship swimming: 1. Men and women compete in identical freestyle distances (50m-1500m). 2. IM is held only up to 400m for both genders.
explanation: Both statements are correct. \* Since the inclusion of the women's 1500m freestyle (in 2022 Worlds/2020 Olympics) and men's 800m freestyle, men and women now compete in identical freestyle distances (50, 100, 200, 400, 800, 1500 meters) at World Championships. \* The Individual Medley (IM) events are held for 200m and 400m distances only for both genders. There is no 50m, 100m (except short course), or 800m IM in long-course World Championships.

question: Karnam Malleswari became the first Indian woman to win an Olympic medal in which discipline?
explanation: Karnam Malleswari created history at the 2000 Sydney Olympics by becoming the first Indian woman to win an Olympic medal. She won the Bronze medal in the Weightlifting (69 kg) category. She lifted 110 kg in the Snatch and 130 kg in the Clean and Jerk for a total of 240 kg. Her achievement paved the way for future female Indian athletes like Mary Kom, Saina Nehwal, and P.V. Sindhu.

question: At the 1984 Summer Olympics, P. T. Usha narrowly missed a bronze medal by \_\_\_\_\_\_ of a second.
explanation: At the 1984 Los Angeles Olympics, P.T. Usha (The Payyoli Express) missed the Bronze medal in the 400m Hurdles final by an agonizing one-hundredth (1/100th) of a second. She clocked 55.42 seconds, while Romania's Cristieana Cojocaru clocked 55.41 seconds to take the bronze. This remains the closest any Indian female athlete has come to an Olympic track medal until Neeraj Chopra's gold in field events decades later.

question: Consider the following statements about service rules in table tennis: 1. At 10-10, service alternates every two points. 2. In final doubles, receiver order changes at 5 points.
explanation: Statement 1 is incorrect: In Table Tennis, if the score reaches 10-10 (Deuce), the service alternates after every single point, not every two points. (Normal service change is every 2 points up to 10-10). Statement 2 is correct: In the deciding game (usually the 5th or 7th set) of a doubles match, when one pair reaches 5 points, the pairs change ends, and the receiver order changes (the player receiving serves changes to modify the matchup).

question: What is meant by an "own goal" in football?
options: Goal scored accidentally into own net
explanation: An "own goal" occurs when a player inadvertently causes the ball to go into their own team's goal net (defending net), resulting in a point scored for the opposition. This usually happens during defensive errors, deflections, or miscommunications. The credit for the goal is given to the defending player (labeled as 'OG' in scorecards), not the attacking player, unless the attacker's shot was already on target.

question: In pole vault, how many failed attempts are allowed before disqualification?
explanation: In field events like Pole Vault (and High Jump), an athlete is allowed 3 consecutive failed attempts at any specific height. If they fail all three trials at that height, they are eliminated from the competition. The highest height successfully cleared before elimination is recorded as their result. (Note: Athletes can choose to pass heights, but once they start a height, the 3-failure rule applies).

question: Which of the following terms is specifically associated with the javelin throw?
explanation: In field events like Javelin, Shot Put, Discus, and Hammer throw, the valid landing area is called the Sector. Specifically for Javelin, the term "Sector" refers to the 29-degree angle area where the javelin must land for the throw to be valid. While "Glide" and "Spin" are techniques used in Shot Put (and Spin in Discus), and "Vault" is for Pole Vault/Gymnastics, "Sector" is the standard term for the landing zone in throws.

question: Which of the following is true about "en passant" in chess?
options: Can be done within two moves
Only if capturing pawn is on dark square
explanation: En passant (French for "in passing") is a special pawn capture rule in chess. It can occur only when a pawn moves two squares forward from its starting rank, and lands adjacent to an opponent's pawn. The opponent has the option to capture the moving pawn "as if" it had only moved one square. A crucial condition is that this capture must be done immediately on the very next move. If the player chooses to make another move, the right to capture en passant is lost for that specific instance.

question: Consider statements about Judo competition areas: 1. Inner contest area (8x8m) and outer safety area. 2. Blue uniform called first.
explanation: Both statements are correct. The standard Judo competition area (Tatami) consists of an inner Contest Area (usually 8m x 8m or up to 10m x 10m) and an outer Safety Area (typically 3m wide) of a different color. Regarding uniforms, the judoka wearing the Blue Judogi is usually called first to the mat and stands to the referee's right, while the one in White stands to the left. This color distinction aids referees and spectators in tracking the fighters and scoring.

question: What is the standard width of a singles badminton court?
explanation: The standard width of a Badminton court for Singles matches is 5.18 meters (17 feet). The court excludes the side alleys (tramlines) for singles play. For Doubles matches, the full width of the court is used, which is 6.10 meters (20 feet). The length of the court remains the same for both singles and doubles at 13.40 meters (44 feet). Understanding these boundary lines is critical for service and gameplay rules in professional badminton.

question: Consider statements about high jump: 1. Minimum runway length 15m. 2. Crossbar diameter 15-20cm.
explanation: Statement 1 is correct: The minimum length of the runway for the High Jump is 15 meters, though 20-25 meters is recommended for major competitions to allow athletes to build sufficient speed. Statement 2 is incorrect: The crossbar used in High Jump is roughly circular in cross-section with a diameter of 29mm to 31mm (approx 3 cm), not 15-20 cm. A 15-20 cm bar would be massive and unsuitable. The bar is made of fiberglass or other suitable material and weighs no more than 2 kg.

question: Which statement is correct regarding the Golden Raid and card system in Kabaddi?
options: Green Card leads to 2 min suspension
Fresh toss taken if match tied after 5-5 raids
Red Card results in warning only
explanation: If a Kabaddi match (knockout) ends in a tie, 5 raids per team are allowed. If it is still tied after these 5 raids, a Golden Raid rule applies where a fresh toss is taken, and the team winning the toss performs the raid. The first point scored wins the match ("Sudden Death"). Regarding cards: Green Card is a warning, Yellow Card is a 2-minute suspension, and Red Card is dismissal for the rest of the match (often conveying technical points to opponents).

question: According to official rules, a standard table tennis ball has a diameter of:
explanation: The standard diameter of a Table Tennis ball is 40 millimeters (40mm). This standard was adopted by the International Table Tennis Federation (ITTF) in October 2000 (replacing the older 38mm balls) to slightly slow down the game and make it more viewer-friendly for television. The ball weighs 2.7 grams and is made of celluloid or similar plastic material, usually white or orange with a matte finish.

question: How is the lane width for athletes in races up to 400 meters determined?
options: 1.22 to 1.25 meters wide, marked by 5 cm lines
explanation: In standard athletics tracks (400m oval), the lane width is standardized between 1.22 meters (approx 4 feet) and 1.25 meters. The lanes are separated by white lines that are 5 cm wide. This width is designed to provide sufficient space for an athlete to run comfortably without obstructing others. For races up to 400m (including 100m, 200m, 400m, and hurdles), athletes must stay strictly within their allocated lanes.

question: Which statement about Judo techniques is correct?
options: Match ends if player scores Ippon
explanation: In competitive Judo, if a player scores an Ippon (full point), the match ends immediately, and that player is declared the winner. An Ippon is awarded for a throw with considerable force and control landing the opponent on their back, or for holding an opponent down for 20 seconds, or forcing a submission via choke/armlock. Striking/kicking is forbidden. Shime-waza are chokes (not arm locks), and Hansoku-make is a major penalty resulting in disqualification.

question: How did Sushil Kumar secure India's first Olympic wrestling medal in 56 years?
explanation: At the 2008 Beijing Olympics, Sushil Kumar won the Bronze medal in the 66kg Freestyle wrestling category via the Repechage rounds. After losing his opening bout to Andriy Stadnik (who eventually reached the final), Sushil got a second chance. He won three consecutive bouts in the repechage round, defeating Doug Schwab (USA), Albert Batyrov (Belarus), and finally Leonid Spiridonov (Kazakhstan) to secure the bronze, ending India's 56-year wrestling medal drought since K.D. Jadhav.

question: What is the standard length of the baton exchange zone in a relay race?
explanation: According to updated World Athletics rules (effective since 2018), the baton exchange zone (takeover zone) in relay races (4x100m, 4x400m) is 30 meters long. Previously, there was a 20m exchange zone and a 10m acceleration zone. The rules were simplified to combine these into a single 30m zone where the outgoing runner starts running and the exchange must occur. The check mark is typically placed within this zone to time the handover.

question: Consider statements about pawn promotion: 1. Pawn can promote to any piece except king or pawn. 2. Only to previously captured pieces.
explanation: Statement 1 is correct: In chess, when a pawn reaches the 8th rank (opponent's back rank), it must be promoted immediately to a Queen, Rook, Bishop, or Knight of the same color. It cannot remain a pawn or become a King. Statement 2 is incorrect: The promotion is not limited to previously captured pieces. A player can have multiple Queens (theoretically up to 9) or other pieces on the board simultaneously, regardless of what has been captured.

question: Statements about SAF Games: 1. First edition in Kathmandu, 1984. 2. India hosted more times than any other country up to 2016.
explanation: Statement 1 is correct: The first South Asian Federation (SAF) Games (now South Asian Games) were held in Kathmandu, Nepal in 1984. Statement 2 is incorrect: Up to 2016, India had hosted the games 3 times (Kolkata 1987, Madras 1995, Guwahati/Shillong 2016). However, Bangladesh had also hosted it 3 times (1985, 1993, 2010) during that period. Therefore, India did not strictly host "more times than any other country" (it was tied). Nepal also hosted in 1984, 1999, and 2019.

question: What is the standard height of a football goal post?
explanation: The standard height of a football (soccer) goal post is 2.44 meters (which equals 8 feet), measured from the ground to the lower edge of the crossbar. The width of the goal (distance between the posts) is 7.32 meters (24 feet). These dimensions are standardized by FIFA and IFAB for all professional adult matches. The posts must be white in color and of the same width/depth as the crossbar (not exceeding 12 cm).

question: Which of the following is true about relay races?
explanation: Statement (a) is true: A relay team squad typically consists of 6 members, out of which 4 runners are selected to compete in the actual race (4x100m or 4x400m). The other two are substitutes who can replace a runner in subsequent rounds (heats/finals). \* (b) is wrong: Exchange zone is 30m. \* (c) is wrong: Baton weighs at least 50g. \* (d) is wrong: Baton length is 28-30 cm.

question: Which Union Minister announced Khelo India University Games 2025 in Rajasthan?
explanation: Mansukh Mandaviya, the Union Minister of Youth Affairs and Sports (as per the 2025 cabinet context in the question), announced the Khelo India University Games (KIUG) 2025 to be held in Rajasthan. The Khelo India initiative aims to revive the sports culture in India at the grassroots level. Hosting the University Games in Rajasthan continues the trend of rotating the host state to develop sports infrastructure nationwide.

question: Which statement about Kabaddi history is correct? 1. Comes from Kaunbada. 2. Rules framed in 1918, printed in 1923.
explanation: Statement 2 is correct: The modern rules of competitive Kabaddi were indeed framed around 1918 and first printed/published in 1923 by the Hind Vijay Gymkhana, Baroda. This standardization helped in organizing the All India Kabaddi Tournament in 1923. Statement 1 is incorrect: The word 'Kabaddi' is generally believed to be derived from the Tamil words "Kai-pidi" (holding hands) or similar Dravidian roots, not 'Kaunbada'.

question: At which Olympics did India make its first appearance as an independent nation and win gold in hockey?
explanation: India made its first appearance as an independent nation at the 1948 London Olympics. In a historic final, the Indian men's hockey team defeated Great Britain (the former colonizers) by 4-0 to win the Gold medal. This was India's fourth consecutive Olympic hockey gold, but the first one under the Indian tricolor (not the British flag). Balbir Singh Sr. played a pivotal role in this victory.

question: Elite long jumpers typically use how many strides in their approach run?
explanation: Elite male long jumpers typically use an approach run consisting of 18 to 22 strides (some even up to 24). This allows them to build maximum controllable velocity before takeoff. A longer run-up (around 40-50 meters) is required to reach top speed. Beginners or intermediate athletes might use fewer strides (12-16), but for elite distances, the speed generated in the 20-stride range is crucial for momentum translation into the jump.

question: Statements about India at 2008 Beijing Olympics: 1. Won medals in Shooting, Boxing, Wrestling; first individual Gold. 2. Saina Nehwal participated.
explanation: Both statements are correct. At the 2008 Beijing Olympics, India won 3 medals: Abhinav Bindra (Gold in Shooting, India's first individual gold), Vijender Singh (Bronze in Boxing), and Sushil Kumar (Bronze in Wrestling). Saina Nehwal participated in the 2008 Olympics. She reached the quarterfinals in badminton, becoming the first Indian woman to do so, laying the groundwork for her bronze medal in 2012.

question: The 2018 Intercontinental Cup final was held in which city?
explanation: The 2018 Intercontinental Cup final was held at the Mumbai Football Arena in Mumbai. In the final match, India defeated Kenya by 2-0. Both goals were scored by captain Sunil Chhetri. This tournament is famous for Chhetri's emotional video appeal to fans to come and watch Indian football, which resulted in a sold-out stadium for the subsequent matches.

question: A personal foul in basketball primarily involves:
explanation: In basketball, a Personal Foul involves illegal physical contact with an opponent while the ball is live. Examples include holding, pushing, charging, tripping, or impeding an opponent's progress by extending hands/arms. This is distinct from a Technical Foul (arguing, unsportsmanlike conduct without contact) or Flagrant Foul (violent contact). A player is disqualified after committing 5 (FIBA/NBA) or 6 personal fouls in a game.

question: What critical factor distinguished Vijay Kumar's performance in 25m rapid pistol at London 2012?
explanation: (Note: Vijay Kumar actually won the Silver medal, not Gold. However, based on the provided answer key (b), the question likely frames "Won medal/Silver" or implies a distinguishing podium finish in the exam context). At London 2012, Vijay Kumar won Silver in the 25m Rapid Fire Pistol event. His performance was critical because he held his nerve in the elimination format final, outshooting world champions to finish second only to Leuris Pupo of Cuba.

question: In which city did India win its first-ever Test series against England in 1952?
explanation: India recorded its first-ever Test match victory against England (and first-ever test win overall) in 1952 at the Madras Cricket Club (Chepauk) in Chennai (then Madras). Led by Vijay Hazare, India defeated England by an innings and 8 runs. The hero of the match was Vinoo Mankad, who took 12 wickets (8/55 and 4/53). This victory helped India draw the 5-match series 1-1.

question: India made its Olympic debut in \_\_\_\_\_\_ at the 2016 Games.
explanation: Golf returned to the Olympics in 2016 (Rio) after a 112-year hiatus. India made its debut in Olympic Golf at these games. The Indian contingent included Anirban Lahiri and SSP Chawrasia (Men), and Aditi Ashok (Women). Aditi Ashok, then only 18, created waves by staying in contention for a medal for the first two rounds before finishing 41st. (She later finished 4th in Tokyo 2020).

question: Which is considered a fault in badminton?
explanation: In badminton, it is a service fault if the server's feet are not stationary or if they step on the boundary lines (service court lines) during the serve. The server must stand inside the service court. (a) Hitting the net but going over is legal ("let" only if caught in net). (c) Landing inside is the goal (legal). (d) Returning over the net is legal.

question: Statements about India at 2012 London Olympics: 1. Won 6 medals. 2. Female athletes won two medals for first time.
explanation: Both statements are correct. India won a total of 6 medals (2 Silver, 4 Bronze) at the 2012 London Olympics, which was its best performance until Tokyo 2020. It was the first time two female athletes won medals in a single Olympics for India: Saina Nehwal (Bronze in Badminton) and Mary Kom (Bronze in Boxing).

question: Which sport is linked to the term "Grand Slam"?
explanation: The term "Grand Slam" is most prominently associated with Tennis. It refers to the achievement of winning all four major championships—Australian Open, French Open, Wimbledon, and US Open—in a single calendar year. Winning all four at any point in a career is a "Career Grand Slam." The four tournaments themselves are also individually referred to as Grand Slams.

question: The inaugural IWL final in 2017 was hosted in which city?
explanation: The inaugural edition of the Indian Women's League (IWL) final in 2017 was hosted in New Delhi at the Ambedkar Stadium. The final was played between Eastern Sporting Union (from Manipur) and Rising Student's Club (from Odisha). Eastern Sporting Union won the match 3-0 to become the first-ever champions of the IWL, marking a new chapter for women's football in India.

question: The Summer Olympics held in \_\_\_\_\_\_ featured India's first shooting medal by Rathore.
explanation: India's first individual Olympic medal in Shooting (and first individual Silver medal) was won by Rajyavardhan Singh Rathore at the 2004 Athens Olympics. He competed in the Men's Double Trap event. This achievement was a turning point for Indian shooting, inspiring a generation of shooters like Abhinav Bindra, Gagan Narang, and Vijay Kumar to win medals in subsequent Olympics.

question: Statements about Indian Athletics: 1. Milkha Singh won CWG gold. 2. PT Usha missed bronze in 1980 (1984) by 1/100th sec. 3. NIS located in Pune.
explanation: (Based on the provided answer key (b), which claims 2 and 3 are correct): Statement 1: Milkha Singh DID win Gold at the 1958 Cardiff Commonwealth Games. Statement 2: PT Usha missed bronze in 1984 (not 1980) by 1/100th sec. Statement 3: The Netaji Subhas National Institute of Sports (NIS) is located in Patiala, Punjab, not Pune. (Factually False). Correction/Note: The exam key (b) implies statements 2 and 3 are the intended "correct" set in this specific test logic, despite NIS being in Patiala.

question: When is a fifth set tiebreak played in Grand Slam men's singles?
explanation: As per the new unified rules adopted by all four Grand Slams in 2022, a 10-point tiebreak is played when the score reaches 6-6 in the fifth set (deciding set) of Men's Singles. The first player to reach 10 points (with a lead of 2) wins the match. Previously, different Slams had different rules. Now, it is standardized to 6-6.

question: Match Water Polo terms: A. Center forward 3. Hole set/Pit player (Position) B. Driver 2. Perimeter player (Movement) C. Center back 1. Hole D/Point (Defense) D. Dead-time 4. Period between foul and restart
explanation: Center Forward (A): Also known as the Hole Set or Pit Player (3). Driver (B): A perimeter player (2) specializing in driving toward the goal. Center Back (C): Also known as Hole D (Defense) or Point (1). Dead-time (D): The interval (4) between the whistle for a foul and the restarting of play.

question: Which sport is the term "zugzwang" associated with?
explanation: The term "zugzwang" (German for "compulsion to move") is associated with the game of Chess. It refers to a situation where a player is put at a disadvantage because they must make a move when they would prefer to pass (do nothing). In chess, passing is not allowed. Being in zugzwang means any legal move the player makes will weaken their position or lead to a loss.

question: Statements about National Sports Competitions: 1. National Games began 1924. 2. SGFI holds nationals only for boys. 3. Inter-University tournaments.
explanation: Statements 1 and 3 are correct. The National Games (originally the Indian Olympic Games) indeed began in 1924 in Lahore. Statement 2 is incorrect because the School Games Federation of India (SGFI) organizes National School Games for both boys and girls across various age categories.

question: Statements about badminton strokes: 1. Net Kill (forecourt). 2. Drive (midcourt). 3. Clear (rear court).
explanation: All three statements are correct. In badminton, a Net Kill is played at the forecourt. A Drive is a flat shot typically played from the midcourt. A Clear (or lob) is a high, deep shot played from the rear court to the opponent's back boundary line.

question: Assertion (A): Squash serve must hit front wall... Reason (R): Players must alternate service boxes.
options: Both true, R does not explain A
Both A and R are true
explanation: Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true, but R is not the correct explanation for A. In Squash, a valid serve must hit the front wall above the service line. Additionally, if the server wins a point, they must alternate service boxes. However, the rule about alternating boxes does not explain why the ball must hit the front wall.

question: In which Asian Games did India first win a gold medal in athletics?
explanation: India won its first Gold medal in athletics at the very first Asian Games held in New Delhi in 1951. Lavy Pinto is credited with winning the first gold in the 100m sprint (and 200m).

question: Consider statements about Boxing techniques: 1. Offensive actions. 2. Shadow Boxing. 3. Distances.
explanation: All three are fundamental components of boxing. Offensive actions include punches used to score. Shadow Boxing is a practice method against an imaginary opponent. Distances management (long, medium, close range) is crucial for tactical advantage.

question: Statements about scoring in field hockey: 1. Field goal from inside circle. 2. Penalty corner ball must exit/re-enter. 3. Penalty stroke lifted.
explanation: All three statements are correct. A field goal must be touched inside the shooting circle. During a Penalty Corner, the ball must travel outside the circle before a shot is taken. A Penalty Stroke is typically lifted or flicked as hitting is not allowed.

question: Identify India's first Paralympic medallist and achievement.
explanation: Murlikant Petkar was India's first-ever Paralympic medallist and also the first Gold medallist. He won Gold in the 50m Freestyle Swimming event at the 1972 Heidelberg Paralympics, setting a world record.

question: How many times has India organized the Asian Games?
explanation: India has organized the Asian Games exactly twice: 1951 (the inaugural games) and 1982. Both editions were held in New Delhi.

question: How many players make up a basketball team on court?
explanation: A standard Basketball team consists of 5 players on the court at any one time. The positions typically include Point Guard, Shooting Guard, Small Forward, Power Forward, and Center.

question: Which is correct about National Games in India? 1. Began 1924 Lahore. 2. Renamed in 1940.
explanation: Both statements are correct. The National Games began in 1924 as the Indian Olympic Games in Lahore. The event was officially renamed as the "National Games" in 1940.

question: Statements about Kho-Kho rules: 1. Pole height 1.20-1.25m. 2. Duration 48 mins. 3. Block distance 2.30m.
explanation: All three statements are correct regarding standard Kho-Kho rules. The wooden poles are 1.20 to 1.25 meters high. The total match duration is usually 48 minutes, and the distance between sitting blocks is 2.30 meters.

question: Statements regarding athlete anti-doping responsibilities: 1. Provide whereabouts. 2. Delegate sample collection.
explanation: Statement 1 is correct: Elite athletes must provide accurate whereabouts information for no-notice testing. Statement 2 is incorrect: An athlete cannot delegate sample collection; they must personally submit the sample under observation.

question: Statements about basketball rules: 1. 24-second shot clock. 2. Disqualification only at 5 fouls. 3. Reverse spin dribble.
explanation: Statement 1 is correct: In standard rules, a team has a 24-second shot clock. Statement 2 is incorrect because disqualification can also happen for flagrant or technical fouls. Statement 3 describes a technique, not a fundamental rule.

question: Term for shot going directly through hoop?
explanation: A basketball shot that goes directly through the hoop (net) without touching the rim or backboard is called a "Swish" .

question: First Indian male shuttler to reach World Badminton finals?
explanation: Kidambi Srikanth became the first Indian male shuttler to reach the finals of the BWF World Championships in 2021, where he won the Silver medal.

question: 2017 Asian Athletics Championships city?
explanation: The 22nd Asian Athletics Championships (2017) were held in Bhubaneswar, Odisha, at the Kalinga Stadium. India topped the medal tally for the first time.

question: Inaugural Indian Open Golf Tournament 1964 city?
explanation: The inaugural Indian Open Golf Tournament was held in 1964 in New Delhi at the Delhi Golf Club. The first winner was Australian golfer Peter Thomson.

question: Which athlete won medals in both Asian and CWG for 3000m steeplechase?
explanation: Avinash Sable won a Silver medal at the 2022 Birmingham CWG and a Gold medal at the 2022 Hangzhou Asian Games in the 3000m Steeplechase.

question: Tokyo Olympian C. A. Bhavani Devi is associated with which sport?
explanation: C.A. Bhavani Devi is an Indian Sabre Fencer. She was the first-ever Indian fencer to qualify for the Olympics (Tokyo 2020).

question: Which of the following sports is played on water?
explanation: Surfing is a surface water sport. The other options are land-based sports played on courts or fields.

question: The term Decathlon and Heptathlon are related to which of the following?
explanation: Decathlon and Heptathlon are combined Athletics events. Decathlon is a 10-event competition for men, and Heptathlon is a 7-event competition for women.

question: Volleyball was introduced in the Asian Games in \_\_\_\_\_\_ for women.
explanation: Volleyball for Women was introduced in the Asian Games in 1962 in Jakarta, Indonesia. Men's Volleyball started in 1958.

question: Narcotics are used as doping in sports. Which of the following is an example of narcotics? I. Morphine II. Methadone
explanation: Both Morphine and Methadone are classified as Narcotics under the WADA Prohibited List. They are powerful painkillers that can mask injury pain.

question: A badminton match generally consists games of \_\_\_\_\_\_ points each.
explanation: A standard professional Badminton match consists of the best of three games, with each game played to 21 points using the rally point system.

question: Which country hosted the FIFA World Cup 2022?
explanation: The FIFA World Cup 2022 was hosted by Qatar. It was the first World Cup held in the Arab world and the first "Winter World Cup". Argentina won the title.

question: In India, the first hockey club came up in Calcutta in \_\_\_\_\_\_ .
explanation: The first hockey club in India was established in Calcutta (Kolkata) in 1885-86. This foundation led to the formation of the Bengal Hockey Association in 1908.

question: Prakash Padukone is related to which of the following sports?
explanation: Prakash Padukone is a legendary former Indian Badminton player. He was the first Indian to win the All England Open Badminton Championships in 1980.

question: The Board of Control for Cricket in India was established in the year \_\_\_\_\_\_ .
explanation: The Board of Control for Cricket in India (BCCI) was established in December 1928. It was formed as a society registered under the Tamil Nadu Societies Registration Act. R.E. Grant Govan was its first President and Anthony De Mello was the first Secretary. The formation was a prerequisite for India to be invited to the Imperial Cricket Conference (ICC) and to start playing official Test cricket (which India did in 1932). Today, it is the richest cricket board in the world.

question: Volleyball made its Olympic debut at the \_\_\_\_\_\_ Olympic games.
explanation: Volleyball made its official Olympic debut at the 1964 Tokyo Olympics. Both men's and women's volleyball tournaments were introduced simultaneously. The sport's inclusion was largely driven by its popularity in the host nation, Japan. In the inaugural tournament, the Soviet Union won gold in the men's event, while the host nation Japan (the "Witches of the Orient") won gold in the women's event. It has been a core Olympic sport ever since.

question: In Cricket, which of the following is NOT a type of Fast bowling?
explanation: Chinaman is a term used for slow left-arm unorthodox spin bowling (wrist spin). It is the left-handed equivalent of a right-arm leg spinner. The term originated from Ellis Achong, a West Indian bowler of Chinese descent. In contrast, Bouncer (short-pitched delivery), Leg cutter (fast ball that deviates to the off-side), and In-swinger (fast ball that swings into the batter) are all variations or deliveries associated with fast or seam bowling.

question: Who won the first season of Ranji trophy in 1934-35?
explanation: The first season of the Ranji Trophy (1934-35) was won by Bombay (now Mumbai). In the final match played at the Bombay Gymkhana ground, Bombay defeated Northern India by 208 runs. This victory marked the beginning of Bombay's absolute dominance in domestic cricket, where they went on to win the title a record 41 times (as of 2024). The trophy was donated by Bhupinder Singh, the Maharaja of Patiala.

question: The International Hockey Federation, was founded in Paris in \_\_\_\_\_\_ .
explanation: The International Hockey Federation (FIH) was founded on January 7, 1924, in Paris, France. It was established by Paul Léautey, who became its first president, in response to field hockey being omitted from the 1924 Paris Olympics. The seven founding nations were Austria, Belgium, Czechoslovakia, France, Hungary, Spain, and Switzerland. The FIH is the international governing body for field hockey and indoor hockey, now headquartered in Lausanne, Switzerland.

question: In ice hockey, the fences that surround the ice hockey rink are called as \_\_\_\_\_\_ .
explanation: In Ice Hockey, the low wall or fence that surrounds the ice surface (rink) is called the Boards (or dasher boards). They are typically made of wood or fiberglass frames with a plastic cladding. The boards are topped with shatterproof glass (plexiglass) to protect spectators from flying pucks while allowing visibility. The area where the ice meets the boards is a critical zone for gameplay ("battling along the boards").

question: In basketball there are how many categories of rebounds?
explanation: In basketball, rebounds are broadly classified into 2 categories: \* Offensive Rebound: When a player recovers the ball after a missed shot taken by a teammate (their own team). This gives the team a second chance to score. \* Defensive Rebound: When a player recovers the ball after a missed shot taken by an opponent. This ends the opponent's possession and starts a new offensive play for the rebounding team.

question: The Asian Games Federation was inaugurated in New Delhi in \_\_\_\_\_\_ .
explanation: The Asian Games Federation (AGF) was formally inaugurated in New Delhi on February 13, 1949. Representatives from nine Asian countries signed the constitution. This body was formed to organize the Asian Games, inspired by the vision of Guru Dutt Sondhi and Pandit Jawaharlal Nehru. The federation was later replaced by the Olympic Council of Asia (OCA) in 1982, but the AGF was the original governing body that launched the Asiad movement.

question: The term 'Raider' is related to which of the following sports?
explanation: The term 'Raider' is central to the sport of Kabaddi. A raider is the offensive player who enters the opponent's half of the court chanting "Kabaddi, Kabaddi" (cant). Their objective is to touch as many defenders ("Antis") as possible and return to their own half without being tackled or running out of breath. If successful, the raider scores points; if tackled, the raider is out.

question: \_\_\_\_\_\_ is a type of start which is essential for sprint races in sports.
explanation: The Crouch Start (or block start) is essential and mandatory for all sprint races (up to 400m) in athletics. It allows the runner to generate maximum explosive power and acceleration from a stationary position. The athlete positions themselves on starting blocks in a crouched posture ("On your marks," "Set"). This technique optimizes the forward drive by lowering the center of gravity and utilizing the strong leg muscles (quadriceps/calves) effectively against the blocks.

question: In which of the following Summer Olympics did Neeraj Chopra win the gold medal?
explanation: Neeraj Chopra won the historic Gold medal in the Men's Javelin Throw at the 2020 Tokyo Olympics (held in 2021 due to the pandemic). He recorded a throw of 87.58 meters in the final to defeat Jakub Vadlejch and Vitezslav Vesely. This was India's first-ever Olympic Gold medal in Athletics (Track and Field) and only the second individual gold after Abhinav Bindra.

question: Strokes, Starts and Turns are some of the Techniques of which of the following sports?
explanation: Strokes, Starts, and Turns are fundamental technical components of competitive Swimming. \* Strokes: Refer to the four swimming styles (Freestyle, Backstroke, Breaststroke, Butterfly). \* Starts: The technique of diving into the water from the starting block (or from the water in backstroke) to gain initial momentum. \* Turns: The maneuver used to reverse direction at the wall (e.g., Tumble turn in freestyle, Open turn in breaststroke) without losing speed.

question: In \_\_\_\_\_\_ the All-India Kabaddi Federation came into existence.
explanation: The All India Kabaddi Federation (AIKF) was formed in 1950 to look after the promotion of the game. It organized the first National Kabaddi Championship for Men in Madras (Chennai) in 1952. Later, it was reconstituted as the Amateur Kabaddi Federation of India (AKFI) in 1972/73 to affiliate with the Indian Olympic Association (IOA) and standardize the rules for international competitions like the Asian Games.

question: Pankaj Advani received the Rajiv Gandhi Khel Ratna in 2006. He is associated with which sport?
explanation: Pankaj Advani is a world-renowned Indian player of Billiards and Snooker. He is often referred to as the "Prince of Pune" or "The Golden Boy." He has won over 25 World Titles across both formats (IBSF World Billiards and World Snooker Championships). He is the only player to have won world titles in both the long and short formats of snooker and both the time and point formats of billiards. He received the Khel Ratna (now Major Dhyan Chand Khel Ratna) in 2006.

question: A Kho-Kho playground (or pitch) is \_\_\_\_\_\_ in shape.
explanation: A standard Kho-Kho playground is rectangular in shape. According to the Kho-Kho Federation of India, the measurements for the playing field are 27 meters by 16 meters (for men/women/junior categories). It includes a central lane (with 8 sitting blocks) and two cross lanes. At each end of the central lane, there is a wooden pole. The free zone area behind the pole is also rectangular.

question: In track events, key rules are related to how many phases?
explanation: In track events (specifically sprints), the race and its rules are often analyzed in 4 key phases: \* The Start: Reaction time and block clearance. \* Acceleration Phase: Building up speed (drive phase). \* Maximum Velocity Phase: Reaching and maintaining top speed. \* Deceleration Phase (Speed Endurance): Maintaining form as fatigue sets in near the finish line. Rules govern each phase, such as false starts (Phase 1) or lane infringements (Phase 2-4).

question: Which of the following sports can be played both at individual level and with a team?
explanation: Badminton can be played as an individual sport (Singles: 1 vs 1) or as a team sport (Doubles: 2 vs 2, or in team championships like the Thomas/Uber Cup where individual match wins contribute to a team score). \* Basketball, Cricket, and Hockey are fundamentally team sports requiring a fixed number of players (5, 11, 11 respectively) to form a side; you cannot play a regulation match of "Cricket" or "Hockey" individually 1-on-1 in a formal sense.

question: Which sport brought India its first individual Olympic gold?
explanation: Shooting brought India its first-ever individual Olympic Gold medal. This historic feat was achieved by Abhinav Bindra at the 2008 Beijing Olympics. He won the gold in the Men's 10m Air Rifle event. Until then, all of India's gold medals (8 of them) had come from the Men's Hockey Team. Bindra's victory broke a long-standing barrier and inspired a new generation of Indian athletes.

question: Volleyball was introduced in the Asian Games in \_\_\_\_\_\_ for Men.
explanation: Volleyball (for Men) was introduced in the Asian Games program in 1958, held in Tokyo, Japan. This was the 3rd edition of the Asian Games. It was the first team sport to be added after the initial set (Football, Basketball, etc.). The Japanese men's team won the gold medal in this inaugural tournament. Women's volleyball was added later in 1962 (Jakarta).

question: Which of the following sports was invented by Dr. James Naismith in 1891?
explanation: Basketball was invented by Dr. James Naismith, a Canadian physical education instructor, in December 1891. He invented the game at Springfield College (Massachusetts, USA) as a less injury-prone sport than football to keep athletes active indoors during the winter. He used two peach baskets as hoops and a soccer ball. The original game had 13 basic rules, many of which still form the foundation of modern basketball.

question: Who among the following was part of the gold medal winning Indian hockey team of 1928 Summer Olympics?
explanation: Major Dhyan Chand was a key member (and the star player) of the Indian hockey team that won the Gold medal at the 1928 Amsterdam Olympics. This was India's first-ever Olympic gold. Dhyan Chand scored 14 goals in the tournament, including a hat-trick in the final against the Netherlands. Balbir Singh Sr. played in later Olympics (1948, 1952, 1956). KD Jadhav was a wrestler (1952).

question: The game of judo was invented in the year \_\_\_\_\_\_ .
explanation: Judo was founded/invented by Jigoro Kano in 1882 in Japan. He established the Kodokan (a place for the study of the way) in Tokyo to teach Judo. Kano derived Judo from the ancient martial art of Jujutsu but removed the dangerous techniques, focusing on "maximum efficiency with minimum effort" (Seiryoku Zenyo) and "mutual welfare and benefit" (Jita Kyoei), transforming it into a modern sport and educational system.

question: Where are the headquarters of the National Anti Doping Agency of India situated?
explanation: The headquarters of the National Anti-Doping Agency (NADA) of India are situated in New Delhi (specifically at the JLN Stadium complex). NADA is the national organization responsible for promoting, coordinating, and monitoring the doping control program in sports in all its forms in India. It functions under the aegis of the Ministry of Youth Affairs and Sports and adheres to the World Anti-Doping Agency (WADA) code.

question: The first senior National Handball Championship was held at Rohtak (Haryana) in \_\_\_\_\_\_ .
explanation: The first Senior National Handball Championship (Men) was held at Rohtak, Haryana in 1972. Handball in India was pioneered by Jagat Singh Lohan, an alumnus of YMCA College of Physical Education, Madras. He helped form the Handball Federation of India (HFI) in 1972. The championship marked the formal beginning of organized competitive handball in the country. Haryana won the inaugural Gold medal.

question: Steeplechase is a hurdle race with water jumps came into existence in England around \_\_\_\_\_\_ .
explanation: The modern Steeplechase (specifically the track event) originated in England around 1850 (mid-19th century). It evolved from cross-country steeple-chasing (horse racing from one church steeple to another). Oxford University students adapted it as a footrace. The event was standardized to 3000 meters with fixed hurdles and water jumps for the Olympics much later (1920), but its origins as a formalized track event trace back to the Oxford/Cambridge sports of the 1850s-60s.

question: Who was the first Indian woman to win an Olympic medal?
explanation: Karnam Malleswari was the first Indian woman to win an Olympic medal. She secured the Bronze in Weightlifting (69 kg category) at the 2000 Sydney Olympics. Before her, no Indian woman had stood on the Olympic podium. Her historic lift lifted the spirits of the nation and paved the way for the subsequent success of Indian women in sports.

question: Table tennis made its Olympic debut in \_\_\_\_\_\_ Olympics.
explanation: Table Tennis (Ping Pong) made its official Olympic debut at the 1988 Seoul Olympics (South Korea). Four events were contested: Men's Singles, Women's Singles, Men's Doubles, and Women's Doubles. Since then, it has been a permanent fixture. China has dominated the sport at the Olympics, winning the majority of the gold medals awarded since its introduction.

question: In which sport did Praveen Kumar Sobti won medals at the Asian Games?
explanation: Praveen Kumar Sobti (widely known for playing 'Bheem' in the TV series Mahabharat) was a successful athlete before his acting career. He won Gold medals in Discus Throw at the 1966 and 1970 Asian Games. He also won a Silver in Hammer Throw (1966) and another Silver in Discus (1974). He represented India in two Olympics (1968, 1972) in the hammer and discus throw events.

question: Who was india's first-ever gold medallist at the Asian Games?
explanation: Sachin Nag was India's first-ever Gold medallist at the Asian Games. He achieved this in the inaugural 1951 New Delhi Asian Games by winning the 100m Freestyle Swimming event. Lavy Pinto (Athletics) also won gold in the same games, but Sachin Nag's victory is often cited as the very first gold for the host nation in the swimming pool.

question: The nickname "Flying Sikh" is associated with which Indian athlete?
explanation: The nickname "Flying Sikh" is famously associated with the legendary sprinter Milkha Singh. The title was reportedly bestowed upon him by General Ayub Khan of Pakistan in 1960 after Milkha Singh defeated Pakistan's champion sprinter Abdul Khaliq in a race in Lahore. Ayub Khan told him, "You didn't run today, you flew." Milkha Singh is the only athlete to win Gold in 400m at the Asian Games and the Commonwealth Games.

question: India had a single representative at the Paris Olympics in \_\_\_\_\_\_ .
explanation: India's first participation in the Olympic Games was at the 1900 Paris Olympics. The sole representative was Norman Pritchard, an athlete of British descent born in Calcutta. He competed in athletics and won two Silver medals in the 200m sprint and 200m hurdles.
